Brookline Select Board Meeting - April 14, 2026

Select Board · Brookline · 2026-04-14
tap to load the tape · nothing plays until you do
AI summary — supplements the official record

At the Brookline Select Board meeting on April 14, 2026, discussions focused on roadway safety improvements, particularly for Harvard Street, and the importance of incorporating complete streets policies to enhance safety for cyclists and pedestrians [50:24][64:31][115:28]. The board heard public comments supporting a more pedestrian-friendly design and criticized the prioritization of motor vehicle throughput over safety [60:09][67:12][126:48]. Additionally, the board addressed the need for effective community engagement in the planning process for these projects [123:53][135:05]. No specific votes or decisions were recorded during the meeting, though it emphasized collaboration between the Transportation Board and community stakeholders moving forward [104:54][105:20][236:33].

55:36 My name is Emily Jacobson. Um, I'm a

56:00 improving pavement conditions on our

56:02 streets and um um I applaud the the uh

56:17 2025

56:19 and they should also note on the table

56:21 that I've given you

56:23 um the CIP expenditures on roadways

56:26 since uh 2010

56:29 uh note the significant increase in

56:31 roadway spending in the last few years.

56:34 Even at inflation adjusted numbers this

56:36 represents a sizable increase in

56:38 funding. I think next the select board

56:42 few years several full-time positions

56:43 that had gone unfilled for a long time

56:46 uh have been filled bringing the

56:50 up to six people. It was two when I

56:57 And lastly, I'd like the board to look

57:00 back at the paving work that has been

57:03 done over the last few years. There's

57:05 new asphelt on Washington Street, Beacon

57:07 Street, Pond Avenue. Note that all of

57:10 these projects consisted of a bonded

57:12 wearing course. And a bonded wearing

57:15 course is a road preservation treatment

57:18 that would have fallen outside of the

57:20 previous complete streets policy.

57:23 So that policy really only covered mill

57:26 overlay projects, which have from what I

57:29 have seen only happened on small

57:31 residential streets in the last couple

57:32 years.

57:35 So um second the is the plan that's in

57:39 front of you. Um there were doubts

57:42 expressed about the feasibility of

57:43 parking protected bike lanes on two-way

57:46 single lane roads. Parking protected

57:48 bike lanes are entirely feasible. The

57:50 parking situation for drivers is no

57:52 different from where they must park on a

57:54 two-lane road without any bike lanes.

57:57 Additionally, this kind of layout is far

58:00 from unique. There are numerous examples

58:02 of parking protected bike lanes in this

58:03 configuration in the Boston area and

58:06 I've given you one there from Traymont

58:08 Street in Boston for you guys to look

58:10 at. Thank you.

1:00:00 some close calls with cars. The crash

1:00:04 history validates these concerns. My

1:00:23 the upcoming uh pavement project on

1:01:04 Washington Street in recent years when

1:01:06 that street underwent similar treatment.

1:01:11 and providing parking protected bike

1:01:17 obstruct lanes.

1:01:25 be worth the lifealtering or fatal

1:01:28 crashes they might help avoid.

1:01:34 including lowcost meaningful bicycle

1:01:37 safety improvements on Harvard Street as

1:01:40 part of the upcoming pavement project.

1:04:17 So, who who's next?

1:04:24 All right. Good evening, everyone. My

1:04:26 name is Greg Wheeler. I am a resident of

1:04:28 Precinct 3. Um, and I'm here to talk

1:04:31 about the Harbor Street Paving Project.

1:04:34 Uh, nearly every day I bring my

1:04:36 two-year-old daughter, Ella, to daycare

1:04:38 in Brooklyn Village on my cargo bike. Uh

1:04:42 just last month um there's a stretch on

1:04:44 Harvard Street that we ride. Uh just

1:04:46 last month there were two cyclists

1:04:48 struck by motor vehicles uh during the

1:04:51 time uh my pickup hour in the evening.

1:04:55 [snorts]

1:04:57 I use Harvard Street for everything

1:04:59 including to support dozens of our local

1:05:00 businesses. And I bike because it's the

1:05:03 most efficient and affordable way for my

1:05:05 family to move around town.

1:05:07 Unfortunately,

1:05:10 my daughter's life is threatened every

1:05:12 time we get on a bike due to the lack of

1:05:14 protected bike lanes.

1:05:17 Town leadership has known how dangerous

1:05:18 Harvard Street is for cyclists for

1:05:20 decades and presumably knew it was due

1:05:22 for paving. Yet, it appears the town

1:05:24 failed to contemplate even basic

1:05:25 incremental protective safety

1:05:27 improvements that could be made within

1:05:29 the scope of a paving project. So, I

1:05:31 have two asks for the select board here

1:05:33 tonight. The first is that one, you

1:05:36 approve the construction contract for

1:05:37 this project only with a formal

1:05:39 commitment from the transportation board

1:05:41 and staff to implement no or lowcost

1:05:44 protected bike lanes.

1:05:46 And two, you instruct the the

1:05:48 transportation board to coordinate

1:05:50 priorities from the vision zero vision

1:05:52 zero action plan and the five-year

1:05:55 paving plan so that incremental

1:05:57 improvements can be made during paving

1:05:59 projects in the future.

1:06:00 This is the type of common sense fiscal

1:06:02 and performance management that ensures

1:06:04 the town optimizes every dollar spent

1:06:07 during significant roadway disruptions

1:06:09 like we're going to have this summer on

1:06:10 Harvard Street. In my precinct, I'll

1:06:12 note Longwood Avenue, Aspenwall Avenue,

1:06:14 Kent Street, and St. Paul are all vision

1:06:17 zero priorities that would benefit from

1:06:18 this type of approach. We have policies

1:06:21 and objectives in place already

1:06:23 established.

1:06:25 Now, we're looking to you as leaders to

1:06:27 transparently prioritize and implement

1:06:29 the town's stated goals. I thank you for

1:06:31 your time, your service to the town, uh,

1:06:33 and your consideration of this important

1:06:35 matter. Thank you. [snorts]

1:06:44 Good evening. My name is David Kroo and

1:06:46 I'm the president of Biking Brookline.

1:06:48 Thank you for taking my comments this

1:06:50 evening. Biking Brookline fully supports

1:06:53 the town's pavement preservation policy.

1:06:57 We also fully support the town's

1:06:59 adoption of vision zero, which is an

1:07:02 approach to road safety that says that

1:07:04 the status quo is not okay. It

1:07:07 challenges decades of transportation

1:07:09 policy that prioritize motor vehicles

1:07:12 motor vehicle throughput over safety.

1:07:15 The vis vision zero plan adopted by the

1:07:18 town created a roadmap to achieve its

1:07:20 goals of zero fatalities and serious

1:07:23 injuries by 2035.

1:07:25 A critical part of that roadmap is

1:07:27 assessing overlap between high priority

1:07:30 streets in the pavement management

1:07:31 policy and top priority streets in the

1:07:34 vision zero action plan and determining

1:07:37 where safety improvements can be

1:07:38 integrated into pavement preservation

1:07:41 projects at little or no cost or

1:07:44 additional time. Brookline's adoption of

1:07:47 vision zero reflects its willingness to

1:07:49 change the status quo. This willingness

1:07:52 though is undercut if the transportation

1:07:55 division can decide in its sole

1:07:57 discretion not to include safety

1:07:59 improvements in a road project without

1:08:02 consulting the transportation board and

1:08:04 important stakeholders.

1:08:06 This delegation of authorities suggests

1:08:09 that the decision concerning what safety

1:08:12 improvements, if any, should be included

1:08:14 are relatively little importance and do

1:08:18 not involve weighing of town policies

1:08:20 and priorities. something which is

1:08:22 really the responsibility of the

1:08:24 transportation board. No wonder the

1:08:27 chairs of all three advisory committees

1:08:29 to the transportation board, three

1:08:32 transportation board members and all 15

1:08:35 of the precinct 8 town meeting members

1:08:38 expressed serious reservations about the

1:08:41 Harvard street process. And I'd like to

1:08:44 just digress here for a moment to say

1:08:46 biking Brooklyn would rather not be

1:08:49 spending tons of time interpreting the

1:08:51 intricacies of town's payment

1:08:54 preservation policy and vision zero as

1:08:57 if they're major pieces of federal tax

1:08:59 legislation.

1:09:00 But we felt we had no choice because the

1:09:05 town would not talk to us

1:09:08 and we were rebuffed and told this is a

1:09:11 pavement preservation project. We will

1:09:14 not consider any changes. And I would

1:09:18 really just ask the basic question. Does

1:09:21 the select board really believe that

1:09:23 people in the bicycling community who

1:09:26 ride on Harvard Street every day have

1:09:28 nothing worthwhile that they could say

1:09:31 to the transportation division that they

1:09:33 could consider as they consider what

1:09:37 safety changes could be made? I think

1:09:39 the answer is no. I do think that the

1:09:43 time they spent responding to our emails

1:09:48 and looking over our petition would have

1:09:50 been much better spent if we just sat

1:09:52 together in a room for an hour and

1:09:54 talked about what was possible on

1:09:56 Harvard Street. Thank you very much.

1:10:03 Good afternoon. I'm Philippi Pite from

1:10:05 Addington Road and I bike on Harvard

1:10:09 Street through Harvard Street all the

1:10:11 time. That's where our shops are, our

1:10:13 restaurants, the bayas, my eyeglass

1:10:16 place, the opticians, shoe repair,

1:10:19 everything. It's always there. You know,

1:10:22 is it safe? You know, I'm a big man. My

1:10:27 daughters are grown. Uh I take the risk.

1:10:30 Is it safe for a 12y old, a 15 year old?

1:10:34 No, it's not. So, if we don't make it

1:10:36 safe, we're going to have more cars

1:10:38 spewing fumes on the street, more cars

1:10:41 causing traffic.

1:10:43 Uh we're sending more money to Putin's

1:10:45 wars and Ayatollah's wars. Just uh we

1:10:49 should be reasonable. Let's make it

1:10:51 safer for bikers, not just uh old people

1:10:54 who don't mind risking their lives.

1:10:57 Let's get it done.

1:11:06 Patty, are you do you want to make a

1:11:07 comment? We're running out of time.

1:11:11 Well, not signed up. Can I

1:11:13 Pardon me?

1:11:14 I have not signed up. I was not aware.

1:11:16 Okay. Why don't we do two more comments?

1:11:19 Why don't you go and then Patty and then

1:11:22 we'll close this.

1:11:23 Okay.

1:11:23 Segment.

1:11:24 Um, hi, my name is Luis Pension. Could

1:11:27 you speak into closer to the microphone?

1:11:29 Okay. Yeah. Um [clears throat] I'm gonna

1:11:31 read my notes. Um because like Marilyn

1:11:33 Monro, I cannot memorize a text. My

1:11:36 resemblance with Marilyn Monro stops

1:11:38 here. Well, I guess I am 36 years old,

1:11:41 the age she was when she committed

1:11:43 suicide. And some people on the street

1:11:45 have called me suicidal for biking down

1:11:47 Howard Street. I am not suicidal. I try

1:11:50 very hard to come home to my kids alive.

1:11:53 And it's why I'm here tonight. Another

1:11:56 reason is that I'm conscious. Well,

1:11:58 sorry. Uh, so why am I riding my bike? I

1:12:02 can drive my large secure SUV that would

1:12:04 plow through most of the other street

1:12:06 occupants in the event of a crash. One

1:12:09 of the reason that I ride my bike is

1:12:11 that I'm conscious that I'm going to

1:12:14 pollute the air that people around me

1:12:16 breathes. I'm going to take up most

1:12:18 parking space and I'm going to take

1:12:21 those packing space from people who

1:12:23 actually need them and don't have a

1:12:25 choice. I'm going to wear up the road

1:12:27 with my large SUV and it's going to cost

1:12:29 more money to taxpayer. I'm going to

1:12:31 take more space on the road and I may

1:12:33 block or slow down emergency vehicle.

1:12:37 So most of the time I choose to be kind

1:12:40 and respectful to my neighbors and I

1:12:42 bike despite the risk despite the risks.

1:12:45 I want to show my kids that I am

1:12:47 thinking of my neighbors when I make

1:12:49 choices. I'm not only thinking of myself

1:12:51 and my own safety. I want to show them

1:12:54 that I care for others by making Harvard

1:12:57 street safer. A lot of scared parents

1:12:59 are going to start biking. The traffic

1:13:02 and parking space are going to improve

1:13:04 drastically. There are so many schools

1:13:06 and daycare along Havar Street. Keep

1:13:09 parents safe, kids safe. Stop making

1:13:12 decisions that are going to make the

1:13:14 problems worsen. Let's stop the

1:13:16 hypocrisy and stop banning bike lane for

1:13:19 lost parking spots. Blocking emergency

1:13:22 vehicle where we all buy bigger and

1:13:24 bigger car that take up all the road and

1:13:26 parking space. I take care of my

1:13:29 neighbor to make sure they are safe.

1:13:31 Time for you to do the same and make

1:13:33 sure that I come home alive to my kids.

1:13:35 Thank you. [snorts]

1:13:44 Good evening.

1:13:49 [clears throat]

1:13:54 since my kids were small decades ago.

1:13:58 And I found that biking, I was an

1:14:14 decadesl long uh [clears throat] bike

1:14:23 bike. It's cheap and fast. Um now in

1:14:36 these reasons and also because I have

1:14:41 for me works extremely well. Um I am

1:14:49 in a time when we have scarce town

1:14:52 dollars and we have a vision zero

1:14:54 [snorts]

1:14:55 plan that contemplates that cost

1:14:59 feasible improvements to town roadways

1:15:05 into pavement preservation projects. I

1:15:11 preservation project and I support it,

1:15:25 project. It feels like it's been sort of

1:15:31 objections that have listed specific

1:15:35 improvements that could be incorporated

1:15:38 into this project but have not been

1:15:40 heated so far. Um and so

1:15:44 we have scarce dollars. There's an

1:15:46 opportunity here to improve a tier one

1:15:50 high priority roadway as defined in

1:16:00 feasible improvements will be

1:16:02 incorporated. So, um I encourage the

1:16:20 also want to um suggest that if the

1:16:26 now don't uh accommodate that, then

1:16:29 maybe the problem is with our written

1:16:33 because this should be the type of thing

1:16:35 on a tier one high priority roadway that

1:16:41 there will be more projects coming the

1:16:45 Street, another tier one roadway. And so

1:16:48 I ask that the select board not set a

1:24:58 uh two things. You have to look at the

1:24:59 electric service and figure out how

1:25:01 you're going to get the power into the

1:25:02 ex service whether it's in front of the

1:25:04 meter, behind the meter. So that's an

1:25:06 electrical engineer. The second thing is

1:25:08 structural. They have to get a

1:25:09 structural engineer look at the existing

1:25:11 supports of the building. Now the panels

1:25:12 themselves are not that heavy, but you

1:25:14 can't just put them on a a building. So

1:25:17 sometimes you have to do some structure

1:25:19 work inside the building. I can't say

1:25:21 that for sure at this building. Um, with

1:25:24 newer buildings, they're obviously solar

1:25:26 ready. You don't have to worry about

1:32:38 section is a bit of an elevated stage

1:32:40 platform and then um that will have a

1:32:44 mobile wheelchair lift to the side so

1:32:46 it'll be fully accessible ADA wise that

1:32:49 is also a tented covering that's over

1:32:51 there. We'll have um some tables and

1:32:54 chairs for seating, some bleachers on

1:32:56 that platform for seating. Then going uh

1:32:59 to the side, that's where the food

1:33:07 to last year. And that is a large

1:33:11 trailer with some indoor seating um

1:33:14 along like also some top seating so

1:33:16 people can fully see the course that

1:33:19 will also have an ADA ramp um on the

1:33:21 front of it. So both both units are

1:33:24 fully ADA compliant. And our restroom

1:33:27 trailer also has a ramp. So that is also

1:33:29 ADA compile um compliant.

1:40:04 Okay. We have another double item here.

1:40:07 Uh first approval of

1:40:10 uh a contract with Laruso for

1:40:14 transportation mobility improvements

1:40:16 Harvard Street in the amount of

1:40:17 $3,182,53850.

1:40:24 I guess that's for parking fair.

1:40:27 Um so who's going to talk to us about

1:40:31 that?

1:40:35 No, not it's not a public hearing.

1:40:37 Commission.

1:40:37 Evening. This is Aaron.

1:40:40 Oh, that's okay. I'm here. I have some

1:40:42 staff here as well. Um, good evening

1:40:45 everyone. My name is Aaron Chude. I'm

1:40:46 the commissioner of public works and I'm

1:40:50 here uh tonight to introduce the Harvard

1:40:52 Street Rehabilitation Project and

1:40:54 explain why staff uh recommend the

1:40:58 select board award and execute this

1:41:00 contract. uh this evening. Uh Harvard

1:41:03 Street is one of Brooklyn's most

1:41:06 important corridors. It serves

1:41:07 residents, businesses, schools,

1:41:09 pedestrians, transit users, cyclists,

1:41:13 [gasps] uh and daily vehicle traffic.

1:41:15 And as you heard earlier, it is a really

1:41:19 important um it's an important corridor

1:41:22 and it is also in very poor condition

1:41:26 and in clear need of rehabilitation.

1:41:28 Uh this project is the direct outcome of

1:41:31 the town's pavement maintenance

1:41:33 management and preservation policy and

1:41:36 the broader asset management framework

1:41:38 that the select board asked DPW to put

1:41:41 in place uh to stop serious decline in

1:41:44 pavement condition townwide. Uh that

1:41:47 policy was created so Brookline could

1:41:49 make a more disciplined, transparent,

1:41:52 and financially responsible approach to

1:41:54 maintaining roads based on condition,

1:41:57 need, and long-term value to the town.

1:42:00 Harvard Street was identified through

1:42:02 that adopted process as part of the FY26

1:42:06 roadway rehabilitation program. It was

1:42:09 presented last year to uh the advisory

1:42:14 committee. it was presented uh to the

1:42:16 select board. Uh the intent was to be

1:42:20 presenting the information in a very

1:42:22 open and transparent um manner. Um but

1:42:27 it is timely and important work. As

1:42:29 roads deteriorate, costs increase, risks

1:42:32 increase, and the town falls further

1:42:35 behind. uh timely maintenance is one of

1:42:38 the most responsible things that we can

1:42:40 do to protect the roadway and the

1:42:43 taxpayers's investment in it. Uh this

1:42:46 project also includes practical safety

1:42:49 improvements where feasible and

1:42:51 appropriate, including improved bicycle

1:42:53 lane visibility and buffering,

1:42:55 intersected daylighting, and strategic

1:42:58 use of flex post to deter illegal

1:43:01 parking, which is a real problem along

1:43:03 this corridor.

1:43:05 It's important to note, however, this is

1:43:06 not a full corridor redesign. It's a

1:43:09 roadway rehabilitation and maintenance

1:43:12 project with practical and admittedly

1:43:15 modest safety enhancements. U I want to

1:43:18 recognize uh that members of the BI

1:43:22 bicycle advisory committee that spoke

1:43:24 earlier, some bike advocates are

1:43:26 disappointed uh that this project does

1:43:28 not go further at this time. And I

1:43:31 understand that disappointment and I

1:43:33 respect it. Uh it reflects a real

1:43:35 commitment to safety and to the future

1:43:37 of this corridor. I apologize if members

1:43:42 of this group didn't feel heard. I had

1:43:45 spoken with uh the [clears throat] uh

1:43:49 representative, the leader of the

1:43:51 organization. We asked for information,

1:43:53 some of which is being incorporated. The

1:43:56 transportation director attended a

1:43:58 meeting yesterday and you know right now

1:44:02 the the suggested plans would eliminate

1:44:05 right turning lanes. It was thought to

1:44:08 be unsafe uh in terms of cyclists um and

1:44:14 would require traffic analysis and would

1:44:17 remove some outdoor dining. none of

1:44:19 which couldn't be um worked through but

1:44:24 would require we think a comprehensive

1:44:27 community engagement process. Um so

1:44:31 moving forward with this project right

1:44:33 now does not preclude important

1:44:35 transportation and mobility work here on

1:44:38 other roads. uh we just feel that the

1:44:40 community engagement process uh would be

1:44:43 required here and is important and we

1:44:45 have many important bicycle and

1:44:47 multimmodal projects underway and will

1:44:50 continue um these and others will

1:44:52 continue through the town's full

1:44:54 planning design funding and public

1:44:56 process for this project. I also want to

1:45:00 note that the staff is recommending

1:45:01 video detection for signal optimization

1:45:05 uh rather than the embedded loops in the

1:45:07 pavement. Uh this current technology,

1:45:09 it's already used on Route 9. It's in an

1:45:12 older form on Beacon Street and uh is

1:45:15 currently in use by Newton Mass DOT.

1:45:18 Staff believe that this is the better

1:45:20 option because it's more reliable,

1:45:22 easier to diagnose problems, can send an

1:45:25 email alert if something is not

1:45:27 functioning, and easier to adjust over

1:45:30 time for current and future operations,

1:45:32 including bike related uh functionality.

1:45:36 I understand the use of video detection

1:45:38 may raise questions. So to be clear, the

1:45:40 system does not record, store, send or

1:45:44 share video. It uses video to detect

1:45:46 traffic movement, converts that

1:45:48 information for signal operations, and

1:45:51 that is the end of the process. There's

1:45:53 no speed information or identifying

1:45:55 information recorded or stored. Um,

1:45:58 finally, staff is recommending approval

1:46:00 for night work for the milling and

1:46:01 paving operations. It's being proposed

1:46:04 to reduce disruption during peak commute

1:46:06 times, maintain daytime access for

1:46:09 businesses and [clears throat] corridor

1:46:11 users, improve safety during

1:46:13 construction. We're also hoping that

1:46:15 it'll help shorten the duration of the

1:46:17 overall work as much as possible. So, in

1:46:20 short, Harbor Street is a major corridor

1:46:22 in poor condition. Uh the project is the

1:46:25 direct result of the town's adopted

1:46:27 asset management policy. It's timely,

1:46:30 necessary, and responsible, and improves

1:46:32 the roadway. Now, it includes safety,

1:46:35 practical safety enhancements, but it

1:46:37 still leaves room for much broader

1:46:41 mobility improvements through the proper

1:46:43 public process in the future. And uh

1:46:47 I'll note that we staffed Vision Zero

1:46:49 Complete Streets and our staff remains

1:46:52 deeply committed to improving

1:46:55 multimmodal uh safety improvements

1:46:58 across the community uh and want to do

1:47:01 that uh in partnership uh with uh

1:47:05 [clears throat]

1:47:06 the various various groups. We have uh

1:47:10 staff here uh the director of

1:47:12 engineering transportation Dan Murphy

1:47:14 our associate director of transportation

1:47:17 and mobility Rob Guptell Dan Martin who

1:47:20 is our transportation uh sustainability

1:47:23 transportation engineer and also Sam DS

1:47:25 who's our deputy director of engineering

1:47:29 um all who care deeply about the work

1:47:31 that they do uh for this community. Uh,

1:47:34 and so with that, if you have questions,

1:47:37 um, they're here and ready to answer

1:47:39 those. And, um, I'll turn it back to

1:47:42 you, chair. Thank you.

1:47:49 said that.

1:49:20 my my

1:49:30 maybe not. So, um, but, uh, we have to

1:55:15 Uh John

1:55:16 uh well I want to thank the people in

1:55:18 the community who have you know raised

1:55:20 questions about the camera u uh aspect

1:55:23 of uh this project which you know let's

1:55:25 keep in mind that this is essentially a

1:55:28 a project to deal with the need for um

1:55:32 uh harvestry to be repaved repaired and

1:55:35 repaved. Um and it's a serious need. Um

1:55:38 and uh there's all kinds of safety

1:55:40 issues that stem from you know a road

1:55:42 that is in a deteriorating condition. Um

1:55:46 and then you know as part of the process

1:55:48 people who are very sort of watchful and

1:55:50 good for them uh noticed that there's

1:55:53 reference to camera technology um in the

1:55:56 contract for the improvements to Harvard

1:55:58 Street. Then the question arose you know

1:56:01 what why would there be the this camera

1:56:03 technology? Well, you heard the answer

1:56:05 to that tonight. Um, there's two ways of

1:56:09 monitoring um, how many cars are backed

1:56:11 up at a light. I'll just use that

1:56:13 example. Um, one is for there to be sort

1:56:15 of a a pad um, under the pavement um, at

1:56:19 the same location as the light uh, and

1:56:23 you measure how many cars and and you

1:56:25 know how long the line is I guess by you

1:56:28 know the weight of the car on the pad. A

1:56:31 different way is to have a camera there

1:56:32 which can actually count cars um and so

1:56:35 on. Um there has been no mention made in

1:56:39 any of this uh of a different use of

1:56:41 cameras that is increasingly common

1:56:44 commonplace and in fact advocated um by

1:56:47 many bicyclists and others um and that

1:56:50 is so-called speed cameras. Um, I I I

1:56:54 won't judge whether someday we might use

1:56:58 speed cameras um as part of our routine

1:57:01 maintenance of traffic in uh Brooklyn,

1:57:04 but I would note that that's entirely

1:57:06 consistent with enforcement of speed

1:57:10 limits and with the goals of Vision

1:57:12 Zero. So, uh, that's just an example of

1:57:14 a camera use that a lot of people would

1:57:17 be for. And, um, I think we better, you

1:57:20 know, at some point deal with all of

1:57:23 these different questions that arise,

1:57:25 um, where cameras are concerned. But I

1:57:28 don't think it's reason enough to put

1:57:30 off this vote on this contract tonight.

1:57:33 Um, and while I just, you know, have the

1:57:35 mic, I want to say something else about

1:57:37 all of the people who spoke um earlier

1:57:39 on, uh, from the interests of people

1:57:41 who, uh, uh, bike on Harvard Street, and

1:57:44 I want to thank them for making their

1:57:46 comments, but I do want to, um,

1:57:49 encourage them, uh, in the future, um,

1:57:52 not to imply uh, that our traffic um,

1:57:56 uh, division, um, our transportation

1:57:58 department, our DPW, um, your select

1:58:02 board um uh are unmindful of safety. Um

1:58:07 that of course we're mindful of safety

1:58:09 and it it is not a open-end shut case

1:58:12 that the overall safety of pedestrians

1:58:16 and bicyclists and people in vehicles.

1:58:20 that the overall safety of that street

1:58:22 would necessarily be enhanced if we took

1:58:26 the parking that exists at the curb and

1:58:29 moved it further into the middle of the

1:58:31 street. And to cite just one obvious

1:58:34 example, as someone who experienced

1:58:37 in a in an unfortunate way um the move

1:58:41 um on Center Street, West Roxberry of

1:58:43 parking towards the middle of Center

1:58:45 Street, West Roberry uh with a bike car

1:58:48 protected bike lane to the right of

1:58:50 parked vehicles. And as someone who has

1:58:53 enjoyed um going to the Cororb

1:58:56 restaurant in West Roxberry for many

1:58:58 years, um having first experienced

1:59:00 Cororup right here in Brooklyn, um uh

1:59:03 having never had any issues, um as soon

1:59:06 as that change took place on Center

1:59:08 Street, within one or two visits to the

1:59:10 Cororb in West Roberry, um the uh

1:59:14 driver's side uh mirror had been knocked

1:59:16 off of our car

1:59:18 um and cost us $300 to repair. Um, it

1:59:21 should surprise no one that you when you

1:59:23 move cars closer to the center of um of

1:59:27 a very heavily traveled um uh vehicle

1:59:30 lane where not only is it heavily

1:59:33 traveled, but the cars move very fast.

1:59:35 That's a safety risk to those getting

1:59:37 out of the car. So, you have to take

1:59:39 that into consideration. Um there are

1:59:41 safety risks to people crossing the

1:59:44 street. Um if if for any reason they

1:59:47 step out or people in a car who step

1:59:49 into a bike lane from the car, um they

1:59:52 didn't have that risk on the passenger

1:59:54 side of vehicles, now they will. Um and

1:59:57 that's an added risk. So all these risks

1:59:59 have to be taken into consideration. And

2:00:02 I think um in fairness to those of us

2:00:04 who think we should move forward on this

2:00:06 contract, it should not be posed um uh

2:00:10 even by the most ardent of uh biking

2:00:12 advocates as an indication that we don't

2:00:14 care about safety. I you know I'll I'll

2:00:17 be honest. I I I kind of resented that

2:00:19 tonight. Um because I know we all care

2:00:21 about safety and I know they care about

2:00:23 safety. But let's have a discussion of

2:00:25 safety that embraces pedestrians and

2:00:29 people in vehicles and people on

2:00:31 bicycles and recognizes the reality of

2:00:34 the difference between a tight street

2:00:37 with not much room uh for vehicle lanes

2:00:40 once you start you know doing all these

2:00:42 other changes and a street that is you

2:00:45 know very very wide such as not you know

2:00:47 I won't say it's very very wide but

2:00:48 Beacon Street you know down in the St.

2:00:50 Mary's area. That's a very different

2:00:52 situation. And I'm glad we had um

2:00:55 success with the uh vehicle protected

2:00:57 bike lane there. But that doesn't mean

2:00:59 vehicle protected bike lanes will work

2:01:01 on every street everywhere in

2:01:03 Brooklynite. It it just it isn't true.

2:01:05 And it doesn't mean that people who

2:01:06 raise questions about vehicle protected

2:01:09 bike lanes on a street such as Harvard

2:01:11 Street are unmindful of safety. Um just

2:01:14 not true.

2:01:15 Thank you,

2:01:17 David.

2:01:19 So I I have a few questions. So first,

2:01:21 when who precisely came up with the

2:01:23 plans for which markings to make, which

2:01:27 markings not to make, where to install

2:01:30 flex posts, where not to install flex

2:01:32 posts. Can you provide maybe a timeline

2:01:35 for when these decisions were made, who

2:01:38 provided input into these decisions?

2:01:41 Sure. Sure. I'm I'm Dan Murphy, director

2:01:43 of engineering and transportation and uh

2:01:45 we started the design I believe in

2:01:47 November December and uh Apex uh is our

2:01:50 consultant and they do they do the

2:01:52 markings plan and they will stamp them

2:01:54 at the end when we're finished. Um but

2:01:56 they basically come up with uh what we

2:01:59 can do what improvements they recommend

2:02:01 to make. Um the town staff look at that

2:02:04 as well. Um, if I going through this now

2:02:07 with a new PMP

2:02:09 P policy, I would certainly build in a

2:02:12 step to bring it to uh BAC back and uh

2:02:15 get their input as well. Um, and I'll I

2:02:18 hope that's what comes out of the whole

2:02:19 prioritization progress process. U, but

2:02:22 we do we look at all safety

2:02:23 improvements. We had other things going

2:02:25 on where we had we're putting in seven

2:02:27 new pairs of our RFBs and we uh built

2:02:31 that in. The big the big thing I want to

2:02:33 just point out is we start a year ahead

2:02:35 and really look at the sidewalks, look

2:02:37 at all the ramps. We want to make sure

2:02:38 all the ramps are brought up to code. Uh

2:02:41 so we and we prepare. We got a big a

2:02:43 nice grant from uh Masdot for these

2:02:45 RFBs. So we needed to basically

2:02:47 incorporate making sure everything was

2:02:48 ready for them. Um we go through all the

2:02:51 sidewalks. We did ton of work last year.

2:02:53 We did water service um installations in

2:02:56 preparation and did let everybody know

2:02:58 that we were doing this ahead of the

2:03:00 PMPP project. But I I do it's really uh

2:03:04 Apex comes up with ideas. We review them

2:03:07 and the step that I failed and I I will

2:03:09 do again I will do going forward is to

2:03:11 make sure that back has input on these

2:03:13 PMPB projects to make to make the

2:03:15 changes that we can on the markings

2:03:17 plan. The good thing about the markings

2:03:18 plan is that it's the last thing. So we

2:03:20 do have time uh to adjust those and

2:03:23 we've gone through that. uh uh Rob

2:03:25 Duptel and I myself went to the back

2:03:28 meeting last week and uh heard heard

2:03:30 comments and we received the comments

2:03:32 previously and we worked to incorporate

2:03:35 uh most of them. Uh there's some that we

2:03:37 can't that need community outreach but

2:03:39 uh that was really the process we went

2:03:41 through on this.

2:03:42 So So there was some degree of community

2:03:44 outreach you're saying? Well, I I mean,

2:03:47 we didn't reach out to we I guess I

2:03:50 didn't take these plans to back formally

2:03:53 and uh I think that going forward on

2:03:55 these on these PMPP projects, I will

2:03:58 certainly want to build that in and make

2:04:00 that formal. It's our first time through

2:04:02 on this, we're looking at the

2:04:04 prioritization uh program and how to

2:04:06 come up with all the all the priorities

2:04:08 with the transportation board. And one

2:04:10 of the things we'll definitely build

2:04:11 into that is for the PNP projects, we

2:04:13 want to have a milestone in the middle

2:04:15 of design, the early design, uh, when we

2:04:17 get the maybe the initial cut by our

2:04:20 designer, uh, to have a have everybody

2:04:22 have a look and weigh in with input

2:04:24 because I do see on and it's page 334 of

2:04:28 our materials the uh, FY26

2:04:31 transportation and mobility mobility

2:04:34 improvements scope of work and it

2:04:37 expressly mentions uh, that there'll the

2:04:39 installation of signage and pavement

2:04:41 markings and that there will be flex

2:04:43 post installation which can be used for

2:04:47 uh enhanced bike safety of course. And

2:04:50 then on page 344 in the section

2:04:53 regarding pavement markings there is a

2:04:56 specific uh subclause talking about how

2:05:00 bike lanes and bus lanes will be marked

2:05:02 with methylmethacrylate.

2:05:06 And so that tells me that within the

2:05:08 scope of this contract, there ought to

2:05:10 be some room for uh incorporating

2:05:15 some degree of uh bike safety

2:05:18 uh mechanisms. And the reason this

2:05:20 concerns me a little bit in terms of the

2:05:22 timing, I understand that we are

2:05:24 technically reviewing a contract and

2:05:27 that these processes are not necessarily

2:05:30 one and the same, but this is a high

2:05:33 ticket item. We're talking about uh

2:05:35 three over $3.1 million. And I worry a

2:05:39 little bit about whether this is the

2:05:41 most efficient way to spend uh these

2:05:45 dollars. I wouldn't want to see a

2:05:47 situation where we are repaving a

2:05:50 significant lengthy street only to then

2:05:53 have to do it again when we look at it

2:05:55 through a vision zero lens and yet

2:05:58 another time when we look at it through

2:06:00 a accessibility lens. uh that would seem

2:06:04 to be spending more money than

2:06:05 necessary. So to the extent that we can

2:06:08 have the this additional community input

2:06:12 incorporated into this process and of

2:06:14 course it won't be perfect because some

2:06:17 safety mechanisms would be outside the

2:06:19 scope of the contract but even within

2:06:21 the language that I'm seeing there is

2:06:24 some allowance for input and for bike

2:06:28 lanes and for flex post installation

2:06:30 etc.

2:06:32 just pauses.

2:06:33 No, it's okay. Chaz had something you

2:06:35 want to add.

2:06:36 Well, I just I think it's I think it's

2:06:38 it's important too to think about how

2:06:40 these all of these policies are meant to

2:06:41 interact um and how we are building that

2:06:44 process out. And as Dan pointed out, I

2:06:46 do think there's it's it's important to

2:06:48 develop these levels of input. And it's

2:06:50 important to set those expectations

2:06:52 accordingly, right? In the sense that

2:06:55 Yeah. Right. I I think one of the big

2:06:56 comments that we heard from the

2:06:57 community and I understand where it

2:06:58 comes from is, hey, the contract says

2:07:00 you could do this work. why aren't you

2:07:01 doing it? And the answer is not, well,

2:07:03 it's because, you know, we we don't want

2:07:05 to or, you know, because we don't think

2:07:07 that's there there's value in having

2:07:09 those discussions. There is, but that

2:07:12 process requires so much more effort to,

2:07:16 you know, when you start changing how

2:07:17 the striping is designed, when you start

2:07:20 putting in,

2:07:22 you know, parking protected bike lanes,

2:07:23 that is a significant shift in how the

2:07:25 road is used. And that then triggers,

2:07:27 you know, you're getting right now a lot

2:07:30 of comments from the community saying,

2:07:31 "Hey, you know, we we really want

2:07:33 protected bike lanes. That's a, you

2:07:35 know, vehicle protected bike lanes.

2:07:37 That's a good idea. You know, we're

2:07:38 we're getting that feedback." I think if

2:07:40 you were to shift your priorities and

2:07:42 say, "Okay, we're going to put them in

2:07:44 next meeting," you'd have 100 people

2:07:46 saying, "Don't don't you dare." You

2:07:48 know, absolutely. So I I think this is

2:07:51 this is a really tough situation where

2:07:53 you have very zealous adv advocacy on

2:07:55 all sides and that's why we did the work

2:07:58 that we did and the work that Paul and

2:08:01 Mike Samman did you know last year to

2:08:03 build not just the pavement management

2:08:05 plan but think about how it intersects

2:08:07 and interacts with vision zero and with

2:08:11 um complete streets because we don't

2:08:13 want to give up on complete streets. We

2:08:15 don't want to give up on those projects,

2:08:16 but we also hear the feedback of the

2:08:19 community, which is not enough was

2:08:20 getting done. And so, we have to find a

2:08:23 way, if we can't make everyone happy, we

2:08:24 at least have to find a way to balance

2:08:26 those competing interests. Pave more

2:08:28 roads, don't let the roads fall apart,

2:08:30 think about complete streets, think

2:08:31 about safety, think about vision zero.

2:08:34 All of those things can be intentioned

2:08:36 with each other, and we're trying to

2:08:37 harmonize them as best we can. And that

2:08:39 is going to result in things where

2:08:42 people are going to get 70% of what they

2:08:44 want and it's going to feel like a loss.

2:08:46 Um, and I understand that and I

2:08:48 understand the frustration there. But

2:08:50 you also don't want to be in the

2:08:51 situation where Cambridge is where every

2:08:53 time a board flips, you know, right? I

2:08:55 think last this the Cambridge City

2:08:57 Council last at their last meeting, the

2:09:00 composition of the council has shifted

2:09:02 54 in favor of bike lanes to 54 against

2:09:05 bike lanes several times. And so now

2:09:07 they're taking a vote to scrap a

2:09:09 proposal to scrap a bike lane. Um it's

2:09:13 [laughter] just and and you know staff

2:09:15 have thrown up you know I feel very

2:09:17 badly for the staff in Cambridge who've

2:09:19 just kind of thrown up their hands and

2:09:20 said how on earth how on earth are we

2:09:22 supposed to do this you know like the

2:09:24 the process you know you know improving

2:09:27 the process is really important and

2:09:29 making sure the process is right. So I

2:09:30 take your feedback. You know, I think

2:09:32 it's good feedback, but I also think

2:09:33 it's then important to say we build

2:09:36 these policies to ensure consistency of

2:09:38 outcomes. Um, and when we say, well,

2:09:42 we're going to bend it because this is

2:09:43 an important thing or we need to, you

2:09:45 know, we need to look at this another

2:09:47 way, then you're saying that the

2:09:49 policies and the processes you put in

2:09:51 place are malleable and are subject to

2:09:53 lobbying to get what thing what people

2:09:55 want. um when what we're trying to do is

2:09:59 solicit that input, but also be able to

2:10:01 provide the consistency of outcome and

2:10:02 move forward on it. So, I get that it's

2:10:04 a hard balance to strike. And I think to

2:10:06 Dan's point, we're going to continue to

2:10:08 tinker with that and figure out how we

2:10:09 can make people feel heard and respected

2:10:12 um while also acknowledging that there

2:10:14 are competing voices in the community

2:10:16 and we're trying to synthesize what we

2:10:17 hear. Well, I'm not trying to suggest

2:10:19 that any one particular voice be heard,

2:10:22 but it sounds like the process was work

2:10:25 with a consultant Apex and then staff

2:10:28 reviews Apex's recommendation. And I'm

2:10:31 not sure why we would want to farm out

2:10:34 these decisions to Apex rather than

2:10:38 incorporating community input from

2:10:40 anywhere. So I think but again the

2:10:43 question is

2:10:45 incor you know listening to the

2:10:46 community getting community feedback

2:10:48 which is what you know again Dan and Rob

2:10:50 did you know I think people felt it was

2:10:52 late in the process but there were

2:10:54 changes made as a result of that input

2:10:56 um building that in earlier to the

2:10:58 process is important but I think you

2:11:00 have to set expectations too which is

2:11:02 it's you know if we were to do this as a

2:11:06 re as not just a restriping but a change

2:11:08 in use we would be having community

2:11:10 meetings we would be having all the all

2:11:12 the public feedback and before you know

2:11:14 it the project the you know we're trying

2:11:16 to get this project done over the summer

2:11:17 all of a sudden you're talking about

2:11:18 something that stretches into fall and

2:11:20 winter you're talking about and while

2:11:22 the road is deteriorating and so I think

2:11:24 we've got to come up with a balance

2:11:26 between this idea you know we we want to

2:11:28 be solicitus of public feedback we want

2:11:30 to try and incorporate it but we don't

2:11:32 want everything to then resort you know

2:11:35 come back to this you know design by

2:11:38 committee and the potential for gridlock

2:11:40 that that creates right at a certain

2:11:42 point. We have to say we hire

2:11:45 transportation experts to make these

2:11:46 decisions. They should be making those

2:11:48 decisions well informed with feedback

2:11:50 from the community, but it's not

2:11:52 necessarily a situation where it's

2:11:54 designed by committee.

2:11:57 Paul,

2:11:58 yep. Um

2:12:00 that was a good job. Um yeah, explaining

2:12:02 all all that what you said. But I I do

2:12:05 want to first I want to thank uh Dave

2:12:07 and Emily and everybody who's come

2:12:08 forward um to to speak about uh bic

2:12:12 bicycle safety. Um as you know I've been

2:12:15 a strong supporter of it uh with

2:12:17 Washington Street. Um I've advocated for

2:12:21 uh for the bridal path. Um and frankly I

2:12:25 was supportive of changing Chestnut

2:12:27 Hill. Um you know Chestnut Hill is

2:12:29 actually a great example of what can

2:12:30 happen. We it got so derailed that

2:12:33 nothing is now happening. Um I I do I

2:12:36 think it's important to put into context

2:12:38 what's going on this board not

2:12:40 particularly this board but the select

2:12:41 board I think it was two years ago year

2:12:44 almost two years ago

2:12:44 year and a half yeah

2:12:45 year and a half ago which meant it

2:12:46 started when I joined the board and I

2:12:48 can I'll tell you after how that

2:12:51 happened but um you know this is this is

2:12:54 a policy that that was uh complete

2:12:57 streets which was actually in the way it

2:12:59 had great intentions it had initially

2:13:01 been launched it had never been changed

2:13:03 changed. Um, and I I actually spoke with

2:13:07 the chair of the board who had approved

2:13:08 it at that time. Uh, and I said, "Has

2:13:10 this thing ever been touched?" He said,

2:13:11 "Nope, and it's probably time to revisit

2:13:13 it." And one of the problems with

2:13:14 Complete Streets, it had great

2:13:16 intentions, but it was holding up

2:13:17 maintenance of our roadways. And our

2:13:19 roadways were deteriorating further and

2:13:21 further uh because we didn't have the

2:13:23 ability to just go repave a road um uh

2:13:26 because it had to go through this entire

2:13:28 process and redesign uh for complete

2:13:31 streets. So Mike Sandman and I uh met

2:13:34 with uh the uh DPW commissioner,

2:13:37 engineering, I think uh the the

2:13:40 transportation board chair was there and

2:13:42 others and consultants. Um and we came

2:13:44 up with this this modified uh policy set

2:13:48 which was complete streets and pavement

2:13:50 management. And that those policies were

2:13:53 modified adopted by this board um the

2:13:56 select board and adopted by the

2:13:58 transportation board. there policies

2:13:59 that are in place. Um, and under those

2:14:01 policies, there's kind of two tiers.

2:14:04 There there's tiers of those roadways

2:14:06 that are priorities for complete streets

2:14:08 redesign. Um, it takes a significant

2:14:11 amount of investment to do that. It's

2:14:13 not cheap. Um, but it's important. It

2:14:15 needs to be done. The community

2:14:16 engagement needs to be done properly and

2:14:18 we know that when it isn't done

2:14:19 properly, things get derailed. Uh, like

2:14:21 what happened with Chestnut Hill. Um

2:14:24 there's also a category of roadways that

2:14:26 are important that have a lot of

2:14:28 traffic. Um and we need to maintain them

2:14:31 not only for the safety of of uh

2:14:33 cyclists but also for the safety of

2:14:35 automobiles and pedestrians. Um and and

2:14:38 Harvard Street has fallen has had fallen

2:14:40 into that um has falls into that

2:14:43 category. And the design of the policy,

2:14:45 and frankly this board, the

2:14:47 transportation board could revisit the

2:14:48 policy, but the design of the policy is

2:14:51 to allow the max maximizing the limited

2:14:54 funds that we have to repave and fix as

2:14:58 as many miles of roadway as we can. And

2:15:02 that's what's going on here. um you know

2:15:05 if we were to delay the process at this

2:15:09 point um to do the community engagement

2:15:12 which CHZ had has mentioned it's not

2:15:15 just the bicycle advocates which I

2:15:18 really appreciate their advocacy but

2:15:20 we're going to hear from seniors we're

2:15:21 going to hear from pedestrians we're

2:15:23 going to hear from businesses um we're

2:15:25 going to hear from folks that uh go down

2:15:28 Harvard Street by automobile and it's

2:15:30 going to be a gigantic effort. it always

2:15:33 is. So to take something and just run it

2:15:35 by just the bicycle advisory committee

2:15:37 frankly isn't fair. Um if we're going to

2:15:39 run a review of a repaving project um

2:15:43 you know to the extent that you're

2:15:45 talking about you should have to do it

2:15:46 by every commission that has a say in

2:15:48 it. Um and frankly that would not be a

2:15:50 good use in utilization of our very

2:15:53 limited capital funds. I think we I

2:15:56 think I asked Melissa it was five

2:15:58 million I think is what's in our is in

2:16:00 our CIP for roadways and I believe the

2:16:04 commissioner has told us that we're

2:16:05 about 100 million in the hole. So

2:16:08 that'll tell you how far behind we are.

2:16:10 Um so while I I really appreciate and I

2:16:13 look forward to biking with Biking

2:16:14 Brooklyn again on their when it gets

2:16:16 warmer it's getting warmer now to go on

2:16:21 cycling more with my children. Um there

2:16:26 make making sure that we're maintaining

2:16:27 our roadways and our investments um for

2:16:34 doing these mill and overlays. So um and

2:16:41 supportive of the contract

2:16:44 likely.

2:19:25 going to say uh they're going to do for

2:56:32 would say that I certainly have the

2:56:33 ability and the the I know people in all

2:56:36 the different um precincts in Brooklyn.

2:56:38 Yeah.

2:56:38 And work with them, but I wouldn't say

2:56:40 that I have, for example, a strong

2:56:44 network specific in any one area.

2:56:47 Yeah.

2:56:47 But

2:56:47 it's diverse.

2:56:48 I would say for sure.

2:56:49 Yeah. Okay. Thank you. [snorts]

2:56:53 [clears throat]

2:56:54 Okay. Any other questions?

2:56:55 I'm good.

2:56:57 If you want to be. No, I'm happy to stay

2:57:09 party.

2:57:14 Okay. Is George Cole on?

2:57:16 He said who's going to be here tonight,

2:57:18 but I don't see him online.

2:57:22 Okay. And was there another Wasn't Mike

2:57:23 Sand? Mike, but Mike said he's not

2:57:25 available till after 9:30, so we agreed

2:57:27 that he would go to a future date.

2:57:29 Okay.

2:57:30 Bernard and I talked about it and Mike

2:57:31 agreed.

2:57:32 Yeah. Okay. Um, then let's move on to

2:57:35 our next item or our next interview,

2:57:37 which is for the uh the arts commission,

2:57:41 right?

2:57:42 Yes. And our candidate is online

2:57:45 and we have

2:57:48 Candon Baron

2:57:50 online. Okay.

2:57:52 Yes.

2:57:52 Hi. Please introduce yourself and tell

2:57:55 us why you're interested in the arts

2:57:56 commission.

2:57:59 Good evening, Chairman Green and the

2:58:01 select committee. Um, my name is Candon

2:58:03 Barrett.

2:58:05 I've been a Brooklyn resident for a

2:58:07 total of over 30 years. Um, I grew up in

2:58:11 Brooklyn and graduated Brookline High

2:58:13 School. I went to Heath school for uh

2:58:16 elementary

2:58:18 and then uh as an adult I lived in

2:58:20 Brooklyn uh most recently the last 11

2:58:24 years here in Kulage Corner. Um, I've

2:58:27 spent 30 years uh in the biotech

2:58:30 industry and um last summer I stopped

2:58:35 working and I'm now interested in giving

2:58:39 back to the community that I've lived in

2:58:41 and loved for all these years. And um so

2:58:45 when I noticed that um the town of

2:58:48 Brooklyn was looking for somebody uh to

2:58:51 help with the um commission uh for arts,

2:58:55 um I thought that was something that I

2:58:57 might be able to contribute to. For the

2:58:59 record, I've also applied to the um

2:59:02 conservation commission.

2:59:05 I'm a scientist by training. Um I spent

2:59:08 the last 30 years in biotech uh across

2:59:11 the river in Cambridge primarily. Um the

2:59:15 last 12 years in the gene therapy space.

2:59:18 I'm very well connected with the

2:59:20 adjoining industries of Harvard, MIT

2:59:24 um and um you know some of the uh

2:59:27 biotech industries that are living and

2:59:31 participating in our community here in

2:59:32 Brooklyn. Um but where I think you were

2:59:37 asking chairman about what skills I

2:59:39 might bring to the table. So my hobbies

2:59:43 um have always included art and also um

2:59:48 ecology uh and um I get a lot of

2:59:52 inspiration on the art side from two

2:59:55 places. One is the Isabella Stewart

2:59:57 Gardner Museum where I find it to be

3:00:00 almost like a second home to me. And um

3:00:05 as you might know, Isabella Stewart

3:00:07 Gardner opened that palace. Uh she built

3:00:10 and opened it about a hundred years ago

3:00:12 with the intention of bringing um art

3:00:16 into uh the community and opening up her

3:00:21 um home. The palace was actually built

3:00:23 as a a museum so that the community

3:00:26 could come and visit. Uh at first it was

3:00:29 free and even today it's a very small

3:00:31 amount um that you u pay to get in. Um

3:00:35 and she just filled it with all kinds of

3:00:38 art and artifacts from around the world

3:00:40 and I've always been inspired by that. I

3:00:43 also take inspiration from uh the city

3:00:46 of Key West um that has done some very

3:00:50 interesting things in in their city uh

3:00:53 with respect to public spaces

3:00:57 um including sidewalk art that um

3:01:01 they've done and uh they've done murals.

3:01:04 Um they've done poetry in in the

3:01:07 sidewalks. Um they've done sculptures.

3:01:11 There's a very famous sculptor down

3:01:13 there, John Martini, and he does a lot

3:01:15 for the city there, and they have a

3:01:17 number of public um displays of his art,

3:01:20 and he's donated his uh very large iron

3:01:23 sculptures. And you know, we do that

3:01:26 here in Brooklyn. We have the turkeys

3:01:27 and um we have murals and um we have

3:01:32 these beautiful painted benches now, but

3:01:35 I think we can do more. Um, we have a

3:01:38 number of open storefronts that could

3:01:41 benefit from just some temporary

3:01:43 displays.

3:01:45 Um, I'm not sure what we're doing with

3:01:46 our local artists and with our schools,

3:01:49 but I see that there might be some

3:01:51 possibilities of uh encouraging them to

3:01:55 come together and doing some projects

3:01:57 together.

3:01:59 Um, I think the art commission also has

3:02:02 some leanings towards cultural

3:02:05 diversity. And I'll point out that I was

3:02:08 born in Iran. Uh, I have many Jewish

3:02:10 friends. I have many friends here in

3:02:13 Brooklyn um who are Catholic or

3:02:15 Christian or Jewish or of uh, you know,

3:02:18 from all walks of life. Um, and so I've

3:02:22 always felt comfortable standing in the

3:02:24 middle of a lot of different cultural

3:02:26 aspects of um, society here. And that's

3:02:30 one of the things I love about Brookline

3:02:32 is the diversity here because I also

3:02:34 lived in Weston for a number of years

3:02:35 and it wasn't nearly as fun to live

3:02:37 there to be honest. So um, so that's me.

3:02:42 Maybe I'll just stop there and let you

3:02:44 um, ask your questions.

3:02:47 Okay. Okay. Well, you know, you answered

3:02:49 the question that I had, but maybe you

3:02:52 can elaborate on, you know, what what is

3:02:53 what is a big bodacious idea that you

3:02:56 think that Brookline ought to think

3:02:59 about incorporating into our our art

3:03:01 scene?

3:03:04 Well, some of the things that we might

3:03:05 consider, um, so in Key West, there's

3:03:09 this concept of one human family, and to

3:03:12 me that resonates, um, very much, uh,

3:03:15 for us here in Brooklyn. Um we have so

3:03:19 many different um people from different

3:03:21 parts of the world and different

3:03:23 cultural and um and religious beliefs.

3:03:27 And um you know for me uh unifying theme

3:03:31 is the one human family. And some of the

3:03:34 things that the city of Key West has

3:03:36 done is um they have for example mosaic

3:03:39 inlay one human family design in some of

3:03:44 the important um centers where they have

3:03:48 um you know in the sidewalk in the

3:03:50 ground. Um they have sculptures for

3:03:54 that. And then they they did uh before

3:03:57 Governor Dantis overturned that, they

3:04:00 had painted um some of their crosswalks

3:04:03 uh with rainbow colors um in the center

3:04:07 right where Duval Street has uh you know

3:04:11 basically where they do the New Year's

3:04:13 um celebrations with the drag queens and

3:04:15 things. And I thought that was for me

3:04:19 the big audacious idea was to do

3:04:21 something along those lines. uh was to

3:04:24 see if that conversation has happened in

3:04:27 terms of um what we're doing with

3:04:29 sidewalks or with our crosswalks um and

3:04:32 if there's room for that, if there's

3:04:34 interest for that. So, I'm new to city

3:04:38 governance. Um, and you know, obviously,

3:04:41 uh, I'm still just beginning to network

3:04:44 with the people who are kind of, uh,

3:04:47 driving a lot of these these, uh, ideas

3:04:49 and decisions. But, um, I'm always happy

3:04:53 to listen to what other people have to

3:04:55 bring to the table. But, but that was

3:04:57 one of the things that I thought we

3:04:59 might talk about here in Brooklyn.

3:05:01 I think I think that's a great idea.

3:05:03 It's a it's a theme that could be part

3:05:06 of our art scene, but also a theme that

3:05:09 relates to how we view ourselves as a

3:05:11 community. So anyway, any other

3:05:15 selective members have questions? John,

3:05:18 uh, I just I just want to say I hope

3:05:21 that, um, if you, uh, join the arts

3:05:23 council that you will bring a little bit

3:05:24 of Key West to Brooklyn. [laughter] And

3:05:27 I'm I'm I'm envisioning the day when,

3:05:29 um, just like they do in Key West. Uh we

3:05:32 all gather at the western most point I I

3:05:35 hope I'm reciting it correctly of uh of

3:05:39 the peninsula, our little peninsula, and

3:05:41 we to all toast the setting of the sun

3:05:44 um rockously

3:05:46 because that's one of the charms of Key

3:05:48 West. Um there's a lot of other Key

3:05:50 West. Yeah.

3:05:50 As a matter of fact, if you look down

3:05:51 Harvard Street from Kulage Corner, you

3:05:53 can during certain time of the year, you

3:05:56 can watch the sunset right down there.

3:05:58 I've done that before. It's a lot of

3:06:00 fun. See, it's obvious she's already

3:06:02 thinking about this.

3:06:03 Yeah, we could do that. Yeah. [laughter]

3:06:04 But, um, thank you for applying for the

3:06:06 arts commission.

3:06:07 Yeah,

3:06:08 of course.

3:06:09 And we don't have the problem of a

3:06:11 governor who doesn't like the one human

3:06:14 family.

3:06:17 Anyway, um,

3:06:20 [clears throat]

3:06:21 did you want

3:06:22 Yes. Just looking at your uh resume, I

3:06:26 see that your background is heavy in the

3:06:28 sciences and you [clears throat] do list

3:06:31 arts as a hobby. Not that this is a

3:06:33 prerequisite, of course, but I'm

3:06:35 curious, have you had any art

3:06:37 affiliations of some kind or other

3:06:40 experience serving on art councils or

3:06:43 art groups or anything you want to

3:06:44 elaborate on there?

3:06:47 I have not. I mean, I've taken a number

3:06:49 of um continuing ed art classes at

3:06:53 Brooklyn High School and I've taken some

3:06:56 painting classes and some mosaic

3:06:59 classes, but I have never been on any

3:07:01 other councils. Um the only other boards

3:07:04 I've been on are um nonprofit sort of

3:07:07 biotechy type of boards. Um, and then

3:07:10 and I continue to serve on a nonprofit

3:07:12 board right now, but I have not been on

3:07:15 any other art councils and things like

3:07:17 that, but that's a very relevant

3:07:18 question.

3:07:21 Uh, Michael,

3:07:23 um, I know one of the recommendations of

3:07:29 attend a meeting of uh, the

3:07:32 [clears throat] commission or the public

3:07:33 body that you're interested in joining.

3:07:35 So, I'm wondering first of all if you

3:07:37 have attended a meeting of the Brooklyn

3:07:39 Commission for the Arts. Um, and if you

3:07:41 have, what was your impression of the

3:07:44 work or before the com before the

3:07:48 commission or um the the way in which

3:07:52 the meeting was conducted?

3:07:54 Yeah, thank you for asking that

3:07:56 question. I um to be honest with you,

3:07:58 I've only just recently applied and I

3:08:01 haven't attended a meeting yet and I I

3:08:04 have poked around the website a bit. Um

3:08:08 what I noticed is that they haven't

3:08:10 really posted much on that website since

3:08:13 2024.

3:08:15 Uh last year seems to have been kind of

3:08:18 gone silent a little bit. So I was a

3:08:20 little bit curious about that. Um, and I

3:08:23 definitely if there's anybody that's on

3:08:25 the call right now that's on that

3:08:27 commission, I would love to arrange to

3:08:30 um meet with someone and and talk to

3:08:32 them even if it's not a formal meeting.

3:08:35 Thank you.

3:08:38 Okay. Uh, Paul.

3:08:39 Yeah. One very quick question. First,

3:08:41 thanks for coming forward and you have

3:08:43 some uh some really interesting ideas.

3:08:45 So, you mentioned that you're also

3:08:47 applying for another commission. Um, and

3:08:50 so can I ask you, do you have a

3:08:51 preference?

3:08:54 Um, I have a lot of time on my hands

3:08:56 right now and I I don't necessarily have

3:08:58 a preference. I I would like to serve

3:09:00 the community where there's the greatest

3:09:02 need and where um the committee might

3:09:06 see me fit the best. You you know who

3:09:10 you have and who your other candidates

3:09:12 are. Um, for the conservation

3:09:14 commission, you know, obviously I'm a

3:09:16 scientist. I'm a hobby uh tree grower.

3:09:20 I've grown some mahogany saplings in Key

3:09:22 West and here in Brooklyn, I'm growing

3:09:26 um for better or for worse uh oak and

3:09:29 and maple trees here in in my porch for

3:09:33 whoever wants them. But uh other than

3:09:36 that um you know I I love both things.

3:09:40 That's my other hobby. I also belong to

3:09:42 the association for the preservation of

3:09:44 Cape Cod and um I so as a scientist I've

3:09:48 always been interested in ecology and um

3:09:51 preservation of our coastlines and

3:09:53 waterways and um making sure that we

3:09:56 have in the urban communities enough

3:09:58 trees um so that the the birds

3:10:02 especially the migrating birds have a

3:10:03 place to feed and and to nest. Um and

3:10:08 we're seeing a lot of uh deterioration.

3:10:10 We're seeing a lot of trees getting cut

3:10:12 down in cities

3:10:14 uh in Cape Cod as well as Key West as

3:10:17 well as here in Brooklyn. And we don't

3:10:19 always replace them. If we do, sometimes

3:10:22 they're much much much younger trees

3:10:25 that don't always survive. And they

3:10:27 obviously don't have the same canopy as

3:10:29 a much taller, older tree. So, it takes

3:10:33 many more years um to reestablish the

3:10:35 shade and the canopy and and the the

3:10:38 diversity that you um have with an older

3:10:41 tree. So, that's another area that's of

3:10:44 interest to me, but um I'll leave it up

3:10:47 to you guys to see where you see me and

3:10:49 where you have the greatest need because

3:10:51 that's something that I don't really

3:10:53 know.

3:10:53 Okay. Thank you.

3:10:54 Great. Thank you. Okay. Thank you. We

3:10:58 appreciate your application and uh we

3:11:01 will make our decisions at a later

3:11:03 meeting. Um, but uh, thank you for

3:11:06 coming.

3:11:07 Thank you, chairman.

3:17:36 agenda are is the special town meeting

3:17:40 warrant articles, uh, which is a public

3:17:42 hearing since it's the first time we've

3:17:47 Mooney is at the podium is going to uh

3:17:52 article or these two Warren articles

3:17:54 really.

3:17:55 Good evening, Select Board. Thank you,

3:17:57 Chair Green. Meredith Mooney, economic

3:17:59 development director. Um, so here

3:18:01 tonight to present an update on the

3:18:03 Chestnut Hill uh commercial overlay

3:18:05 zoning package of warrant articles.

3:18:07 There are two articles. One is a

3:18:09 proposed set of amendments to the zoning

3:18:10 bylaw that would incorporate the

3:18:12 proposed overlay zoning. And then the

3:18:14 second is um authorization from town

3:18:18 meeting for the select board to enter

3:18:20 into a memorandum agreement as well as a

3:18:22 tax certainty agreement with city realy

3:18:24 uh which is the largest property owner

3:18:26 in the Chesnut Hill commercial area of

3:18:28 the Chesnut Hill office park which is at

3:18:30 1280 to 1330 Boilston Street.

3:18:32 Okay. And by the way, I'd like to have

3:18:34 the hearing for both warrant articles at

3:18:36 the same time unless anyone objects.

3:18:40 Okay, good.

3:18:41 Okay. Um, so for the purpose of this

3:18:43 evening, the select board um

3:18:48 has already held a public hearing before

3:18:49 the decision was made to move forward.

3:23:18 Okay. So, as Meredith mentioned, City

3:23:21 Realy is the largest uh property owner

3:23:24 uh in the study area. They're

3:23:25 represented in this screen by the uh

3:23:27 large yellow uh block on the western

3:23:41 area um and then curves around the very

3:23:45 edge

3:23:47 of their site. Next slide.

3:23:54 Uh they also own that parcel but it's

3:23:59 Okay, good.

3:24:01 Okay. And the next slide we can skip the

3:24:03 timeline. We've gone over this. Okay. So

3:24:06 within the MOA, uh there is a

3:24:08 description of the project that they

3:24:11 intend to build and the phasing. Uh it

3:24:13 consists of three projects. Buildings A

3:24:15 on the right, B and C. Uh A is a 14story

3:24:18 building that includes

3:24:21 retail, a hotel, condos, parking, two

3:24:24 and a half floors above ground and one

3:24:26 floor below ground. Uh there's a large

3:24:28 plaza between building A and building B.

3:24:30 Uh building B has two floors of retail,

3:24:33 two floors of parking, and eight floors

3:24:35 of medical office. And building C has

3:24:38 six floors of resident of rental

3:24:41 apartments and one floor, one floor of

3:24:44 parking above and one floor of parking

3:24:46 below ground.

3:24:48 Okay. Um

3:24:52 the planning department uh contracted

3:24:55 with RKG associates to come up with a

3:24:57 fiscal model to analyze the uh benefits

3:25:01 to the town. Um we have plugged in

3:25:04 parameters from city realy in terms of

3:25:06 the program that they intend to build as

3:25:08 well as market parameters for data like

3:25:11 uh the square foot rental for medical

3:25:14 office or retail space or the expected

3:25:16 rentals for the units and the costs of

3:25:19 the condos. Um and then use our budgets

3:25:22 in order to estimate costs for the

3:25:25 businesses and the residents of this

3:25:27 space. um and have come up with these

3:25:30 ranges of estimates um based on their

3:25:34 current design. Um so in the end the net

3:25:37 new tax growth is somewhere between 4.2

3:25:40 and $6.3 million per year of annual net

3:25:45 new tax growth. Uh and that takes into

3:25:48 account both the cost of services such

3:25:50 as schools and fire and police and other

3:25:53 town services as well as the cost uh as

3:25:56 well as the income that we already

3:25:58 received from these four parcels which

3:26:00 is roughly $1.1 million.

3:26:04 Okay.

3:26:06 There was a lot of questions about the

3:26:09 how much commercial um we would get out

3:26:12 of uh this project.

3:26:14 Um,

3:26:16 one of the select boards in uh,

3:26:19 directions for the creation of this

3:26:21 study was to incentivize net new

3:26:23 commercial tax growth. And there are

3:26:25 both mechanisms within the zoning and

3:26:27 the MOA in order to incentivize net new

3:26:31 tax growth uh, in the largest amount

3:26:34 possible. Um, the results of what the

3:26:37 program proposes in terms of the

3:26:38 percentage of the sites that are

3:26:40 commercial tax is measured in two ways.

3:26:43 um one you can measure it just by

3:26:44 comparing the square feet. Um but we've

3:26:47 also uh with the consultation from the

3:26:50 economic development advisory board uh

3:26:52 created a set of effective factors for

3:26:55 particular commercial uses. These are

3:26:57 uses that either benefit the town

3:27:00 economically such as a hotel which

3:27:02 provides excise tax as well as property

3:27:05 taxes or uses which were which reflect

3:27:09 neighborhood preferences such as small

3:27:12 retail or medium-sized retail um for

3:27:16 neighborhood uh access and use.

3:27:23 Okay.

3:27:26 So,

3:27:28 so now I'd like to go into actually it's

3:27:30 a series of four slides that talk about

3:27:32 the contents of the MOA. Uh, and I've

3:27:34 divided them into three sections. Um,

3:27:36 the first is a set of town benefits. Um,

3:27:40 in previous uh conversations we've

3:27:43 talked about the $24 million of

3:27:45 benefits. Most of that, though not all,

3:27:47 is described in this list. Uh it

3:27:49 includes the almost $11 million payment

3:27:53 to the affordable housing trust fund um

3:27:55 which is the largest uh payment set of

3:27:58 payments to the affordable housing trust

3:28:00 fund in its history. The second largest

3:28:02 one we talked about earlier which is the

3:28:04 well tower project on Fiser Hill. Um

3:28:07 there will be 21 units as reflected in

3:28:10 our inclusionary zoning bylaw within

3:28:12 section within building C um available

3:28:16 to people with 50% AMI or below. Um

3:28:20 for the first time we have established a

3:28:24 2% hard construction cost payment to the

3:28:28 town uh for improvements to our

3:28:30 infrastructure both uh transportation

3:28:33 streetscape and public safety

3:28:35 infrastructure. Uh based on the

3:28:36 estimates that we've gotten from city

3:28:38 realy of roughly $600 million in hard

3:28:43 construction costs, we're expecting

3:28:44 approximately $12 million of uh

3:28:48 infrastructure contributions to the

3:28:50 town. Um that will be paid out over the

3:28:52 course of the building projects. Um and

3:28:54 I'll talk about the details of that in a

3:28:57 couple of slides. Um there's also some

3:28:59 smaller uh benefits to the town. There's

3:29:03 uh contributions to nonprofits, access

3:29:05 to conference rooms, uh and they are

3:29:07 exploring the prospect of a business

3:29:09 improvement district. Next slide.

3:29:13 In addition to all those town benefits,

3:29:15 the MOA lays out a number of protections

3:29:17 for the town um that uh protect the town

3:29:21 from various adverse uh uh conditions or

3:29:26 risks um that we want to mitigate uh

3:29:29 that we would like to mitigate. Um the

3:29:33 first thing to note is both the MOA and

3:29:35 the tax certainty agreement will be

3:29:37 registered along the chain of title for

3:29:39 the four parcels um that make up the

3:29:42 city realy properties. Um the MOA will

3:29:46 get registered as soon as all of the

3:29:47 town conditions are met, which is

3:29:49 practically speaking when the AG

3:29:51 approves the town meeting warrant

3:29:53 articles. and the 95-year tax certainty

3:29:55 agreement for all four parcels will be

3:29:58 uh registered with the chain of title

3:30:00 upon delivery of the certificate of

3:30:02 occupancy for the first building. Um and

3:30:07 the the tax certainty agreements

3:30:11 registration is a condition of the MOA.

3:30:14 Um they have also agreed uh not to file

3:30:17 any 40B comprehensive permit

3:30:19 applications for as long as the MOA is

3:30:21 in force. um and the fossil and a number

3:30:25 of the other conditions below

3:30:27 fossil-free fuel buildings, the

3:30:29 transportation demand management plan,

3:30:31 etc. Um are all

3:30:34 uh special permit conditions within the

3:30:38 MOA. Um we have uh addressed concerns

3:30:43 expressed by the neighborhood um about

3:30:45 the possibility that they could uh build

3:30:48 one of the uh buildings or two of the

3:30:51 buildings and not complete the program.

3:30:53 Um so for building B uh which is the

3:30:55 medical office building which they have

3:30:57 said is will probably be the third

3:30:59 building that they pull a building for

3:31:01 permit for and construct. Um we have

3:31:04 introduc introduced penalties for delays

3:31:07 in the medical office building

3:31:08 construction. Um both delay there's

3:31:12 there is um a section of the zoning

3:31:15 which says that they can't get the

3:31:17 building uh the certificate of occupancy

3:31:19 for building C if they build that first

3:31:22 and they haven't broken ground and

3:31:24 delivered proof of financing for

3:31:26 building B. uh and there are additional

3:31:29 penalties for delays in the medical

3:31:31 office building in the case that they

3:31:34 actually break ground but they don't

3:31:36 actually complete the medical office

3:31:38 building within a certain number of

3:31:39 years. Uh and those penalties are

3:31:41 actually based on the assessed value of

3:31:44 the medical office building as the

3:31:47 building permit indicates would be

3:31:49 constructed. Um the construction

3:31:52 mitigation impact is is $400,000 set

3:31:55 aside for any parts of the streetscape

3:31:58 um that are damaged during the

3:32:01 construction process. Um and the

3:32:03 following

3:32:05 uh details the hotel occupancy, the

3:32:07 unvital vital unbundled parking fees,

3:32:10 the waste management plan for the

3:32:12 demolition um and the building cost

3:32:14 fundings are all separate items within

3:32:16 the MOA.

3:32:18 Next slide.

3:32:21 Um the third slide is a set of community

3:32:24 protections and benefits. So these are

3:32:26 all issues that um are uh either brought

3:32:29 up by the community members or offered

3:32:31 by city realy in order to make the site

3:32:35 more welcoming, accessible, and uh

3:32:38 integrated with the neighborhood. Um the

3:32:41 the first two are really uh the

3:32:44 significant uh uh neighborhood benefits.

3:32:48 Um we've agreed uh and city realy has

3:32:51 agreed that there will be no vehicular

3:32:53 access except for access by emergency

3:32:55 vehicles from Heath Street. Um that is

3:32:57 actually in the MOA and subsequent to

3:33:00 the MOA we also put this in the zoning.

3:33:03 Um

3:33:04 the pedestrian access path from Heath

3:33:06 Street to Boilston Street include um

3:33:09 access to the large side open space

3:33:11 directly from uh both ends as well. So

3:33:15 access to the um to the ground floor

3:33:19 retail will all be available um through

3:33:23 pedestrian access paths uh spanning the

3:33:26 property.

3:33:31 Sure.

3:33:33 Okay. Um the third item, the landscape

3:33:36 buffer at the western edge was a a

3:33:39 relatively recent addition uh uh that

3:33:42 came from a suggestion from the

3:33:43 neighborhood. Um but there is in

3:33:46 building C a pretty a long wall that

3:33:49 extends the the most of the length of

3:33:52 the western border of the site. Um and

3:33:54 there was concern about what the

3:33:56 presence of a sevenstory building wall

3:33:58 would be. So they have agreed uh within

3:34:00 the 40oot setback that we have uh

3:34:03 established on the western edge to uh

3:34:06 fill that with dense vegetation as well.

3:34:09 Um and um so that will that will both

3:34:12 serve as a sound buffer and a visual

3:34:15 buffer um to both the neighbors within

3:34:18 Brookline as well as the condo buildings

3:34:20 in Newton. Uh the public EV chargers,

3:34:23 the shuttle um and the signage dimming

3:34:25 after midnight are also conditions with

3:34:28 the MOA. Um and as Janice mentioned be

3:34:31 uh earlier during public comment, there

3:34:33 are additional neighborhood benefits

3:34:35 that are under negotiation between city

3:34:37 realy the uh precinct 14 and 15 TMM

3:34:40 which Janice is uh leading and the town.

3:34:46 [snorts]

3:34:47 Next slide. Um I wanted to just spend

3:34:50 one a few seconds on what there were

3:34:53 possible uses for the 2% hard

3:34:56 construction costs, the $12 million. Um,

3:34:59 these include uh the opportunity for

3:35:01 traffic improvement design and

3:35:03 implementation. Um, hopefully in

3:35:05 coordination with much larger

3:35:07 contributions by Mass DOT. Um, a blue

3:35:10 bike station is mentioned explicitly,

3:35:12 various streetscape improvements, trees,

3:35:14 benches, etc. Um and also public safety

3:35:18 in infrastructure is an option if we

3:35:20 choose to use it. Um which would include

3:35:24 uh potentially a ladder truck and

3:35:26 equipment.

3:35:28 Next slide.

3:35:31 The 95-year tax certainty agreement is

3:35:34 uh intended to protect the town from two

3:35:37 particular uh risks. Um the first risk

3:35:40 is that if a building is sold to a

3:35:43 taxexempt organization, um they are

3:35:46 exempt from pl paying property taxes to

3:35:48 the town. Uh the 95-year tax certainty

3:35:51 agreement says that if a building is

3:35:54 sold to a taxexempt organization, then

3:35:56 the owner must continue to make payments

3:35:58 to the town equal to what the property

3:36:00 taxes would have been. Uh there was a

3:36:02 question raised at the AC meeting last

3:36:04 night about whether those taxes would

3:36:07 increase over time and the answer is yes

3:36:09 because the calculation of those taxes

3:36:12 would be tied to the assessed value of

3:36:14 the building itself.

3:36:16 Uh the second protection built into the

3:36:18 95-year tax certainty agreement um and

3:36:21 this is I think not part of previous tax

3:36:24 certainty agreements that we have

3:36:26 engaged in is that is a protection for

3:36:28 the excise taxes of the hotel. Um so

3:36:31 that if the hotel is converted to a

3:36:33 different use then there's a calculation

3:36:35 that's performed based on um average

3:36:38 hotel excise taxes across the region. Um

3:36:42 and the owner must continue to make

3:36:43 payments to the town equal to those

3:36:46 hotel excise taxes. I've included in

3:36:49 your con for your convenience in the

3:36:50 slide deck a list of all of the terms

3:36:52 that have been established within the

3:36:54 MOA and where they're referenced in case

3:36:56 you need to know what the hotel and

3:36:58 condo buildings exist uh or what we

3:37:02 define as the town. Um and that is all I

3:37:05 have for any questions

3:37:09 and back to you Meredith.

3:37:11 Very good. Any questions from the select

3:37:13 board?
